FT_IC1.evidence: US-ABCD Study (9-to-12-year-old early adolescents from 21 US catchment sites; ABCD 4.0 release, baseline 2016-2018, 1-year follow-up 2017-2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM: social media (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter) at year 0 and year 1 (Table 1, reported β=-0.12 to -0.16, p<.05 cross-sectionally). Qualifying smartphone: texting (year 0 and year 1, β=-0.15 to -0.09), video chatting (year 0 and year 1), watching/streaming videos (YouTube) (β=-0.18 to -0.24). Also TV and video games measured separately. Applies B1: YSTS paper reporting separate β for SM, texting, video chat, and app-category (videos, games) per year.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with SM and smartphone modalities (independent variables) vs MIND diet score (dependent variable, continuous). Both prospective (year 0→year 1) and cross-sectional (year 1) regression coefficients reported (β with 95% CI and p-values in Table 2).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients extracted: Table 2 reports β, 95% CI, and p-values for all screen modalities (SM: β=-0.12 to -0.16; texting: β=-0.09 to -0.15; video chat; videos/YouTube: β=-0.18 to -0.24). Categorical analyses also report β with 95% CI.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of US-ABCD data. Multiple linear regression examining prospective and cross-sectional associations between screen time modalities and diet quality (MIND diet score).

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; published 2024 in Academic Pediatrics.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/SM is INDEPENDENT variable predicting nutrition (non-SM outcome); fails criterion (a). Disqualifies EC7 per B18 logic (SM as IV → outcome, not as DV).
notes: All six screen time modalities measured separately with individual coefficients: TV, video games, videos (YouTube), texting, video chat, social media. Meets FT-IC2 via B1 (YSTS with separate SM, texting, video chat, app-category coefficients). Both prospective and cross-sectional models presented.

FT_IC1.evidence: US-ABCD Study (N=9859, Year 3, 2019-2021, 12-13-year-old early adolescents from national sample)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM: social media with SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) scores reported as dependent variable and also platform-specific use measured. Qualifying smartphone: video games (single-player, multi-player) via VGAQ; mobile phone via MPIQ; texting measured separately. Measured modalities: texting, social media, video games, video chatting, browsing internet, TV/movies (Table 2-3). Applies B1: separate coefficients reported for each SM/smartphone modality.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with gender identity (transgender vs cisgender; gender-questioning vs cisgender) as independent variable and screen time modalities as dependent variables. Table 2 and Table 3 report β (95% CI) and p-values for all screen use outcomes including SM addiction scores.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients in Table 2 and Table 3: β with 95% CI and p-values for total recreational screen time (β=1.17-7.85, β=3.41), social media (β=0.42-0.51), video games (β=0.28-0.57), mobile phone (β=0.56-0.60), texting, and other modalities.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of US-ABCD Year 3 data examining associations between gender identity and screen use (recreation and problematic).

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; published 2024 in Annals of Epidemiology.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/SM is DEPENDENT variable (outcome) predicted by gender identity (demographic); however, fails criterion (a) because paper includes only cross-sectional analysis. Fails criterion (c) per B15 carve-out: any cross-sectional demo→SM disqualifies EC7.
notes: Cross-sectional analysis only (Year 3 ABCD). Screen modalities are outcomes (dependent variables) predicted by gender identity. Gender identity is sociodemographic but analysis is not longitudinal; B15 applies (cross-sectional demo→SM disqualifies EC7 if EC7 were to apply).

FT_IC1.evidence: US-ABCD Study (N=9,398 early adolescents aged 11-12 years, Years 2-3, 2018-2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone: bedtime phone use (leaving ringer on, talking/texting on phone at bedtime); bedtime video games; TV/streaming in bedroom (bedtime use). Qualifying SM: time on social media at bedtime. Independent variables report separate screen modalities: TV/movies, video games, music, talking/texting, social media, chat rooms, internet browsing at bedtime (Table 2). Applies B1: separate β reported for each modality per year.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression analyses (logistic and linear) examining associations between bedtime screen use (independent variables, measured at Year 2) and sleep outcomes (dependent variables at Year 3): total sleep disturbance (OR, 95% CI), disorders of initiating/maintaining sleep (β, 95% CI), weekly sleep duration (β, 95% CI) reported in Table 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports OR, β, 95% CI, and p-values for all bedtime screen use exposures: TV/device in bedroom; phone actions; watch/stream videos (β=-0.10 to -0.21, p<.001); video games (β=-0.14, p<.001); music (β=-0.10, p<.001); talking/texting (β=-0.17, p<.001); social media (β=-0.21, p<.001).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis of US-ABCD data (Years 2→3). Regression analyses examining bedtime screen use behaviors and sleep outcomes one year later.

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; published 2024 in Journal of Adolescent Health.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/SM is INDEPENDENT variable predicting sleep outcomes (non-SM outcome); fails criterion (a). Disqualifies EC7 per B18 logic (SM/phone as IV → outcome, not as DV).
notes: Prospective analysis (Year 2→Year 3). All bedtime screen modalities (TV, video games, texting, social media, music, etc.) measured separately at baseline with individual coefficients for sleep outcomes at follow-up. Both logistic regression (sleep disturbance, OR) and linear regression (sleep duration, β) models presented.
